CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is organized in a standard logical order (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Discussion/Conclusion) and the high-level contributions (batch active learning for deep nets using a variational/Fisher/KFAC-based criterion) are stated in the introduction. However the notation and some definitions are unevenly presented (e.g. multiple uses of observed vs expected Fisher, W*, WX/WY not always clearly motivated, and the KFAC block contents are left ‘‘undescribed’’ in §3.2.1), which makes key derivations in §3 and §4 hard to follow. Figures/tables are referenced (e.g. table 1, figures 1–3) but are not shown in the provided text and the manuscript contains repeated/garbled passages and several grammatical/formatting issues (duplicated paragraphs near the end, LaTeX artifacts), reducing readability.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a coherent, practically motivated combination: using variational free energy (Eq.1–2) with an asymptotic MLE Gaussian prior/posterior approximation (Eq.5–8), plus a K-FAC / Kronecker-factored Fisher approximation (Sec.3.2.1) to derive a scalable two-term (training + generalization) batch selection criterion and greedy selection procedure (Eqs.16,19). These elements yield a non-trivial, engineering-ready active learning method for deep CNNs that appears novel in this specific assembly and scaling. However, the core technical ingredients are primarily existing (Graves 2011 variational approach, Martens & Grosse K-FAC Fisher approximation, and MLE/trace-based selection related to Zhang & Oles), so the contribution is an incremental but useful extension rather than a fundamentally new idea.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents a plausible variational/Fisher-based batch active learning approach but relies on a cascade of heuristic and sometimes mathematically questionable approximations (e.g. treating the high‑dimensional MLE as asymptotically Gaussian for deep nets in §3.1, replacing traces of inverses by inverses of traces and the loose upper bounds in Eqs. (12)–(13) and (15)). It also uses predicted labels to form Fisher terms for unlabeled data (§3.5) and ad hoc scaling (γ) without theoretical control. Experiments (only MNIST and USPS, Sec.5) show improvements over simple baselines but omit stronger contemporary baselines (e.g. BALD/CoreSet), statistical testing, and ablations of key approximations, so empirical support is limited. Overall the idea is promising but has nontrivial unsupported assumptions and insufficient experimental validation, so conclusions are only partially justified.
